name: DOD-Prompt-Technical-Approach
description: DoD Technical Approach section - objectives, state of the art, methodology, innovation, risks, feasibility

variants.name: default
variants.text:
You are writing the TECHNICAL APPROACH section of a DoD grant proposal. INPUTS: - {{content}}: The researcher's actual work, publications, and technical expertise. This is your PRIMARY source for all methodology, prior results, and technical capabilities. - {{grant_info}}: The specific DoD grant opportunity description, including agency, BAA/PA number, technical focus areas, and requirements. YOUR TASK: Generate ONLY the Technical Approach section. Do NOT generate the Execution Plan, Military Relevance, Budget, Statement of Work, Abstracts, or any other proposal component. Those are handled separately. SECTION STRUCTURE — cover each of these subsections: 1. TECHNICAL OBJECTIVES - State 3-5 specific, measurable technical objectives derived from the research described in {{content}} - For each objective: what will be accomplished, how success will be measured, and what capability it enables - Objectives should map to the technical focus areas in {{grant_info}} - Technology Readiness Level: state starting TRL and target TRL for each objective with justification 2. CURRENT STATE OF THE ART AND GAPS - Describe the current technical state of the art relevant to the research in {{content}} - Identify specific technical gaps or limitations that this research addresses - Reference prior work from {{content}} that establishes the foundation for this proposal - Do NOT fabricate citations — use works referenced in {{content}} or use phrases like "recent work has demonstrated..." without inventing sources - Position the proposed work as advancing beyond the current state of the art 3. TECHNICAL APPROACH AND METHODOLOGY For each technical objective, provide: a. APPROACH OVERVIEW - High-level strategy for achieving this objective - Why this approach is appropriate and advantageous b. DETAILED METHODOLOGY - Specific methods, algorithms, techniques, or experimental procedures grounded in {{content}} - Use the researcher's actual technical terminology - Mathematical or theoretical foundations where relevant - Design parameters, specifications, or performance targets - Do NOT assume AI/ML methods unless {{content}} describes them - If the research involves computational methods, describe them accurately using terminology from {{content}} c. DATA AND RESOURCES - Data sources, datasets, or experimental materials needed - Data collection, generation, or acquisition strategy - Quality control and validation procedures - Computational resources required d. VALIDATION AND TESTING - How results will be validated and verified - Specific metrics and success criteria with quantitative thresholds where possible - Test scenarios or experimental conditions - Comparison with baseline methods or existing approaches - If applicable: simulation, hardware-in-the-loop, or field testing plans 4. INNOVATION - What is genuinely new about this approach compared to prior work - Distinguish between: new methods, new applications of existing methods, new theoretical frameworks, or new combinations of approaches - Reference the researcher's specific innovations from {{content}} - Do NOT overstate novelty — be precise about what is new and what builds on established methods - If the approach combines techniques from different fields, explain what this combination enables 5. TECHNICAL RISKS AND MITIGATIONS - For each major technical risk: * What could go wrong technically * Why it's a risk for this specific approach * How it will be mitigated * Alternative approach if mitigation fails - Include go/no-go criteria at key decision points - Present as a table or structured list 6. PRELIMINARY RESULTS AND FEASIBILITY - Present preliminary data, proof-of-concept results, or prior work from {{content}} that demonstrates feasibility - For each result, connect it to a specific technical objective - Include quantitative performance metrics where available in {{content}} - If {{content}} includes specific measurements, accuracies, or demonstrated capabilities, present them as evidence of feasibility WRITING GUIDELINES: - Write for expert technical reviewers who may include both academic researchers and DoD scientists/engineers - Use precise technical terminology from {{content}} - Be specific: include actual parameters, specifications, performance metrics from {{content}} - Do NOT reframe the research as artificial intelligence or machine learning unless {{content}} explicitly describes AI/ML as core methodology - Do NOT fabricate preliminary data, performance metrics, or citations not in {{content}} - If {{content}} does not provide sufficient technical detail for a subsection, note what additional information would strengthen it - Emphasize technical rigor and feasibility — DoD reviewers evaluate whether the approach will actually work - Define all acronyms on first use - Do NOT include classified or sensitive information - Do NOT include URLs to external websites ALIGNMENT: - Read {{grant_info}} carefully and identify specific technical focus areas, thrust areas, or capability gaps mentioned - Frame each technical objective as addressing a specific need from {{grant_info}} - Use terminology from {{grant_info}} where it naturally aligns with the researcher's work in {{content}} - Scale technical depth to the mechanism: - BAA full proposal: comprehensive technical detail - SBIR Phase I: feasibility focus, proof-of-concept - White paper: high-level approach, less detail - DARPA: emphasize revolutionary potential and risk/reward DOD EVALUATION CRITERIA TO ADDRESS: - Technical merit: scientific quality and soundness - Innovation: novelty and advancement beyond state of the art - Feasibility: likelihood of technical success - Methodology: appropriateness and rigor of approach OUTPUT FORMAT: Generate well-structured HTML: - Use

for the section title "Technical Approach" - Use

for each major subsection - Use

for each technical objective - Use

for sub-subsections within objectives - Use

for paragraph content - Use , , , ,
, for risk matrices and comparison tables - Use
    /
      for lists - Use for emphasis sparingly - Target approximately 6-10 pages (4,000-6,000 words)
